source_paper_title: Generalized Monotonicity and the Proximal Point Algorithm

context:
Section 3 (Question 3.8, page 10) and reiterated in Section 6 Conclusion and Future Work (page 29).

Question 3.8. Let \(A : H \to 2^H\) be maximally monotone. Suppose that \(\mathrm{zer}\,A = \{\bar x\}\). Are the following two statements equivalent?
(i) \(A\) is metrically subregular at \(\bar x\) for \(0 \in A\bar x\).
(ii) \(A^{-1}\) is Lipschitz continuous at \(0\) with a positive modulus.
title: Equivalence of metric subregularity and inverse Lipschitzness for maximal monotone operators
background_latex:
Let \(H\) be a real Hilbert space with norm \(\|\cdot\|\). Let \(A:H\rightrightarrows H\) be a maximally monotone (set-valued) operator, i.e., its graph \(\mathrm{gra}\,A:=\{(x,u)\in H\times H: u\in A(x)\}\) is monotone and maximal w.r.t. graph inclusion.

The set of zeros of \(A\) is \(\mathrm{zer}\,A:=\{x\in H: 0\in A(x)\}\). Assume \(\mathrm{zer}\,A=\{\bar x\}\) is a singleton.

For a set \(C\subseteq H\), the distance from \(x\) to \(C\) is \(d(x,C):=\inf_{y\in C}\|x-y\|\). For a point \(y\in H\) and a set-valued mapping \(F:H\rightrightarrows H\), define \(d(y,F(x)):=\inf_{v\in F(x)}\|y-v\|\).

Metric subregularity of \(A\) at \(\bar x\) for \(0\in A(\bar x)\) means: there exist constants \(\kappa\ge 0\) and \(\delta>0\) such that for all \(x\in B[\bar x;\delta]:=\{x\in H:\|x-\bar x\|\le \delta\}\),
\[
d\bigl(x, A^{-1}(0)\bigr) \le \kappa\, d\bigl(0, A(x)\bigr).
\]
Since \(A^{-1}(0)=\mathrm{zer}\,A=\{\bar x\}\), this can be rewritten as \(\|x-\bar x\|\le \kappa\, d(0,A(x))\) for \(x\) near \(\bar x\).

Lipschitz continuity of the inverse mapping \(A^{-1}\) at \(0\) with modulus \(\alpha>0\) means: there exist \(\tau>0\) and \(\alpha>0\) such that whenever \((w,x)\in \mathrm{gra}\,A^{-1}\) and \(\|w\|\le \tau\),
\[
\|x-\bar x\| \le \alpha\,\|w\|.
\]
Equivalently, for all \(w\) sufficiently close to \(0\) and all \(x\in A^{-1}(w)\), the above inequality holds.
problem_definition_latex:
Assume \(A:H\rightrightarrows H\) is maximally monotone and \(\mathrm{zer}\,A=\{\bar x\}\).

Determine whether the following two properties are equivalent:

1) (Metric subregularity at the unique zero) There exist \(\kappa\ge 0\) and \(\delta>0\) such that for all \(x\in B[\bar x;\delta]\),
\[
\|x-\bar x\| \le \kappa\, d\bigl(0, A(x)\bigr).
\]

2) (Inverse Lipschitz at the origin) There exist \(\alpha>0\) and \(\tau>0\) such that for all \((w,x)\in \mathrm{gra}\,A^{-1}\) with \(\|w\|\le \tau\),
\[
\|x-\bar x\| \le \alpha\,\|w\|.
\]

In particular, decide whether 1) implies 2), since 2) implies 1) is already known.

Let \(H\) be a real Hilbert space with norm \(\|\cdot\|\). Let \(A:H\rightrightarrows H\) be a maximally monotone (set-valued) operator, i.e., its graph \(\mathrm{gra}\,A:=\{(x,u)\in H\times H: u\in A(x)\}\) is monotone and maximal w.r.t. graph inclusion.

Assume \(A:H\rightrightarrows H\) is maximally monotone and \(\mathrm{zer}\,A=\{\bar x\}\).

Determine whether the following two properties are equivalent:

1) (Metric subregularity at the unique zero) There exist \(\kappa\ge 0\) and \(\delta>0\) such that for all \(x\in B[\bar x;\delta]\),
\[

\]

In particular, decide whether 1) implies 2), since 2) implies 1) is already known.
location_in_paper: Section 3 (Question 3.8, page 10) and reiterated in Section 6 Conclusion and Future Work (page 29).
open_problem_quote:
Question 3.8. Let \(A : H \to 2^H\) be maximally monotone. Suppose that \(\mathrm{zer}\,A = \{\bar x\}\). Are the following two statements equivalent?
(i) \(A\) is metrically subregular at \(\bar x\) for \(0 \in A\bar x\).
(ii) \(A^{-1}\) is Lipschitz continuous at \(0\) with a positive modulus.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: A counterexample, if it exists, would likely live in an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space or involve a carefully constructed set-valued monotone operator; searching systematically is nontrivial. However, for structured families (e.g., subdifferentials of convex functions, normal cone operators, linear relations) one can algorithmically test subregularity/Lipschitz-inverse conditions numerically on discretizations, giving some scope for automated exploration.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can parameterize candidate operators within computable subclasses (e.g., piecewise-linear monotone maps in \(\mathbb{R}^n\), subdifferentials of convex piecewise-quadratic functions, or monotone linear relations) and use a fitness signal based on estimating local error-bound constants versus inverse-Lipschitz constants. The main limitation is that verifying the properties rigorously (especially in infinite dimensions or for set-valued maps) may require symbolic/analytic arguments beyond numerical testing.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem asks whether, for a maximally monotone operator \(A\) with a unique zero \(\bar x\), metric subregularity of \(A\) at \((\bar x,0)\) implies a Lipschitz property of the inverse mapping \(A^{-1}\) at the origin. In general variational analysis, metric subregularity is a local error bound for the inclusion \(0\in A(x)\), while Lipschitz continuity of \(A^{-1}\) at \(0\) is a stronger stability property of the solution mapping \(w\mapsto A^{-1}(w)\). The direction \(2\Rightarrow 1\) is standard: if \(A^{-1}\) is Lipschitz at \(0\), then \(\|x-\bar x\|\le \alpha\, d(0,A(x))\) follows by taking \(w\in A(x)\) with \(\|w\|\approx d(0,A(x))\). The difficult direction is \(1\Rightarrow 2\), which would upgrade an error bound for the residual \(d(0,A(x))\) into uniform Lipschitz control of all nearby inverse images \(x\in A^{-1}(w)\).

No forward-citation evidence was provided, and among the accessible surrounding literature the equivalence is not stated as resolved. Standard references on solution mappings and regularity (e.g., Dontchev--Rockafellar) explain that subregularity is typically weaker than strong metric regularity (which is equivalent to single-valued Lipschitz localization of the inverse), suggesting that additional assumptions beyond maximal monotonicity and isolatedness of \(\mathrm{zer}\,A\) may be needed for \(1\Rightarrow 2\). Algorithmic works on proximal/warped proximal methods (Rockafellar; B{\`u}i--Combettes; Le--Dao--Th{\'e}ra) and error-bound-driven linear convergence (Drusvyatskiy--Lewis) motivate why such an implication would be impactful: it would allow one to replace inverse Lipschitz assumptions by the more checkable residual error bound when proving linear rates for proximal-type algorithms.

At present, based on the supplied materials, the problem should be regarded as open: it is plausible that metric subregularity at an isolated zero does not by itself guarantee Lipschitz continuity of \(A^{-1}\) at \(0\) without strengthening to (strong) metric regularity or imposing additional structure (e.g., strong monotonicity, polyhedrality, or smoothness/second-order conditions in subdifferential cases). Promising directions are to (i) search for counterexamples among maximally monotone operators with isolated zeros but multi-valued inverses near \(0\), and (ii) identify natural classes (subdifferentials of convex functions with quadratic growth, piecewise linear-quadratic functions, or polyhedral monotone operators) where subregularity is known to coincide with strong metric regularity, thereby yielding \(1\Leftrightarrow 2\) in those settings.

sources.title: Monotone operators and the proximal point algorithm
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces the proximal point algorithm for maximal monotone operators and foundational properties of resolvents and maximal monotonicity in Hilbert spaces.
sources.connection: Provides the classical framework and notation for maximal monotone operators and their inverses/resolvents, within which metric subregularity and inverse Lipschitz properties are studied.

sources.title: Implicit Functions and Solution Mappings
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops variational-analytic theory of metric regularity/subregularity and Lipschitz properties of solution mappings, including relationships among these notions for set-valued maps.
sources.connection: Contains general results comparing metric subregularity of a mapping with Lipschitz properties of its inverse/solution mapping, clarifying what can and cannot be inferred without stronger regularity assumptions.

sources.title: Error bounds, quadratic growth, and linear convergence of proximal methods
sources.theorem_or_result: Connects error bounds/metric subregularity-type conditions to linear convergence of proximal algorithms in convex and variational settings.
sources.connection: Uses metric subregularity/error bounds as a central assumption; helps interpret the open problem as asking when such an error bound yields a Lipschitz inverse for maximal monotone operators.

sources.title: Warped proximal iterations for monotone inclusions
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces warped proximal iterations and warped resolvents for monotone inclusions, generalizing classical resolvent-based methods.
sources.connection: While not directly about subregularity vs inverse Lipschitzness, it extends the algorithmic context (proximal/resolvent methods) where such regularity properties influence convergence rates.

sources.title: Solving non-monotone inclusions using monotonicity of pairs of operators
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops generalized proximal point methods based on monotonicity of operator pairs and establishes convergence under mild assumptions.
sources.connection: Cited by the 2026 paper; provides algorithmic motivation where stronger regularity (e.g., inverse Lipschitzness) would yield linear rates, but does not resolve the equivalence question for maximal monotone operators.
